file_name: Haresta et al. - 2025 - Development of ViScan a mobile application for skin cancer detection using ionic framework and yolo.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLOv10x deep learning model for real-time object detection of skin lesions. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset containing dermoscopy images to classify seven categories of skin cancer/lesions. The system achieved a precision of 84.2%, recall of 86.3%, mAP of 89.2%, and F1 Score of 85.2% in detecting skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes the original development of a mobile application named ViScan for skin cancer detection using the YOLOv10x model and Ionic Framework. It details specific methods including dataset preprocessing (HAM10000), model training, system architecture (Node.js, PostgreSQL, TensorFlow.js), and presents original experimental results with performance metrics (Precision 84.2%, Recall 86.3%, mAP 89.2%, F1 Score 85.2%) and black-box testing validation.

title: Development of ViScan: A Mobile Application for Skin Cancer Detection Using Ionic Framework and YOLOv10x
journal: Journal of Applied Informatics and Computing
brief_summary: This study developed ViScan, a cross-platform mobile application for real-time skin cancer detection using the YOLOv10x deep learning model and the Ionic Framework. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset, which was balanced to 3,500 images across seven skin lesion categories using undersampling and augmentation techniques. System performance was evaluated using precision, recall, mean average precision (mAP), and F1 score, achieving values of 84.2%, 86.3%, 89.2%, and 85.2%, respectively. The application architecture integrates TensorFlow.js for on-device inference, Node.js for backend processing, and PostgreSQL for data storage. Black-box testing confirmed the app's functionality and usability, demonstrating its potential as an accessible, low-cost screening tool for early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Bounding box coordinates and classification labels for seven categories of skin lesions (VASC, AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, MEL).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of the YOLOv10x model for 'real-time object detection' of skin lesions. The model outputs 'bounding box coordinates' and 'detection labels' indicating specific skin cancer/lesion classes. While it involves classifying lesions into discrete categories, the primary task is defined as object detection (localization + classification) using YOLO, evidenced by the use of mAP (mean Average Precision) as a key metric and the output of bounding boxes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of a diverse collection of dermoscopy images of skin lesions. The original dataset contained 10,015 images. For this study, it was balanced using undersampling and oversampling (augmentation techniques like rotation, flipping, zooming, and brightness adjustment) to create a subset of 3,500 images (500 per class). The dataset was uploaded to Roboflow for annotation.

use.training: Used to train the YOLOv10x object detection model. The training subset consisted of 80% of the balanced dataset (2,800 images).
use.validation: Used to validate the model during training. The validation subset consisted of 10% of the balanced dataset (350 images).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model performance. The testing subset consisted of 10% of the balanced dataset (350 images).
use.other: The original dataset (10,015 images) was processed and balanced to create the final subset used for the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (Precision, Recall, mAP, F1 Score) and black-box testing.

HAM10000.notes: The model was trained on a balanced subset of 3,500 images (500 per class) derived from the original HAM10000 dataset. The dataset was split into 80% training (2,800 images), 10% validation (350 images), and 10% testing (350 images). The results demonstrate strong accuracy in skin lesion detection for pre-screening.

architecture_details.framework: TensorFlow.js (converted from YOLOv10x for Node.js environment)
architecture_details.deployment: Node.js server with Express.js and Multer modules; PostgreSQL database for data storage
architecture_details.mobile_integration: Ionic Framework for cross-platform mobile application (Android/iOS)

training_details.dataset: HAM10000 (balanced subset of 3,500 images: 500 per class)
training_details.split: 80% training (2,800 images), 10% validation (350 images), 10% testing (350 images)
training_details.preprocessing: Undersampling of majority class, oversampling via augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming, brightness adjustment) for minority class; resizing to 224x224 pixels; automatic orientation adjustment

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Bounding box coordinates, confidence values, and detection labels

preprocessing_techniques: Undersampling of the majority class to balance dataset distribution
Oversampling of the minority class via data duplication and augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, zooming, and brightness level adjustment)
Resizing images to 224x224 pixels to match YOLOv10x input requirements
Annotation and object detection-based techniques using Roboflow

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as an oversampling augmentation technique for the minority class in the HAM10000 dataset to balance the distribution.

conclusions: The developed ViScan mobile application effectively utilizes the YOLOv10x deep learning model for real-time, accurate detection of skin lesions, achieving a precision of 84.2%, recall of 86.3%, mAP of 89.2%, and F1 Score of 85.2%.

The application demonstrates strong potential as an accessible, low-cost screening tool for early skin cancer detection, particularly beneficial for individuals in remote areas with limited access to medical facilities.

The integration of the Ionic Framework, TensorFlow.js, Node.js, and PostgreSQL creates a secure, cross-platform (Android/iOS) system that ensures reliable data storage and efficient real-time image processing.

Black-box testing confirmed that the application provides a smooth user experience with clear navigation and clear presentation of detection results, validating its usability for self-monitoring skin health.
The study highlights the limitation that while the application aids in early detection and awareness, it serves as a screening tool rather than a replacement for professional medical examinations.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which may introduce population bias as the dataset's demographic diversity is not explicitly detailed in the context.
The dataset was artificially balanced to 500 images per class via undersampling and augmentation, which does not reflect the natural prevalence distribution of skin lesions in clinical practice.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the held-out test split of the training dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to unseen populations or devices.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported performance metrics (Precision 84.2%, Recall 86.3%, mAP 89.2%, F1 Score 85.2%).
The application is explicitly positioned as a screening tool for early detection and awareness, not a diagnostic replacement for professional medical examination.

The system architecture involves sending images to a Node.js server for processing via REST API, which raises potential data privacy and security concerns despite the use of HTTPS and JWT authentication.
The study focuses on software development and black-box usability testing rather than clinical efficacy or user health outcomes.

The model was trained on dermoscopy images, but the application uses the device camera, creating a potential domain gap between training data (clinical dermoscopy) and deployment data (consumer smartphone photography).
